intent: Same source domain (httpd_t) and same target TYPE (default_t) but THREE different object classes: file{read}, lnk_file{read}, chr_file{read write}. Tests that perms are NEVER unioned across classes - one allow line per (sdomain, ttype, tclass) triple. The class is part of the grouping key; perms (and the valid perm vocabulary) differ per class.
_kinds_note: All three TeAllowable. floor_label none (same role, level s0 throughout).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow httpd_t default_t:chr_file { read write }; allow httpd_t default_t:file read; allow httpd_t default_t:lnk_file read; (THREE separate lines - audit2allow keys on the class, so the three classes are never merged)
oracle.audit2allow_R: allow httpd_t default_t:chr_file { read write }; files_read_default_files(httpd_t); files_read_default_symlinks(httpd_t) (the file and lnk_file denials become two separate macros; chr_file stays raw because no interface matched it)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: -R substitutes files_read_default_files() (read_file_perms padding on ALL default_t files) and files_read_default_symlinks() (read on all default_t symlinks) for the file and lnk_file denials. Our expected.te keeps all THREE as exact raw allows per class: file read, lnk_file read, chr_file { read write }. No macro, no perm padding, no cross-class union.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A grouper keyed on (sdomain, ttype) but NOT tclass would try to union file+lnk_file+chr_file perms into one rule like `allow httpd_t default_t:??? { read write };` - which is both semantically wrong (read on a chr_file is a different capability than read on a file) and may be UNCOMPILABLE if it picks one class but includes another class's perms. The class is mandatory in the grouping key. Also tests that chr_file's { read write } is preserved exactly while file/lnk_file keep only read.
